text: There's a new kind of project management I call 'vibe managing' where you fully give in to the vibes, embrace chaos, and forget that the Gantt chart even exists.
Shipped 3 features this week. Deleted 2 of them by Friday. Net progress: 1 feature + the wisdom to know which ones mattered.

I spent 6 months building features nobody asked for.

Not 2 features. Not 5. Fourteen.

Each one felt important. Each one had a beautiful spec doc.

But here's what I learned tracking actual usage across 200 accounts:

3 features drove 91% of daily usage. The other 14? Used by fewer than 2% of users.

We spent 6 weeks removing them. We lost 4% of customers. We gained:
→ 35% faster load times
→ 50% fewer support tickets
→ NPS went from 34 to 61

The lesson: your product isn't what you build. It's what you keep.

What feature did you kill that actually improved your product?

#saas #productmanagement #startups

The best project management tool is a shared Google Doc and a weekly standup.

I will not be taking questions.

3 signs your PM tool is slowing you down 👇

1. You spend more time updating the tool than doing the work
2. Your team has 'workarounds' for basic things
3. Nobody opens it unless someone's watching

We built FlowPM to fix exactly this.

📌 Save this if you're evaluating tools right now.

#projectmanagement #startup #saas #productivity #buildinpublic
Hot take: most project management tools are enterprise software wearing a startup hoodie.
1/ I've onboarded 200+ teams onto project management tools. Here are 7 patterns that predict whether a team will actually USE the tool or abandon it in 3 weeks:

Our monthly recurring revenue hit $42K this month.

Up 12% from last month. Here's the full breakdown:

→ New customers: 34 (down from 41 last month)
→ Churn: 6.2% (worst month ever)
→ Expansion revenue: $8.4K (best month ever)
→ Support tickets: 127 (down 40% since feature cleanup)

The honest truth: growth is slowing. But the customers who stay are spending more.

I'm not sure if that's good or terrifying.

What's your read on expansion revenue vs new logos? Which matters more at $42K MRR?

#buildinpublic #saas #startups
Your Gantt chart is a work of fiction and we both know it.

I analyzed 200 SaaS onboarding flows and here's what the top 10% do differently. The biggest finding: the best onboarding flows don't teach features. They teach workflows. Instead of 'here's how to create a task,' they show 'here's how to run your Monday planning meeting.' The tool becomes invisible. Three specific patterns I saw in every top-performing onboarding: 1) They ask ONE question before showing anything. 2) They pre-populate with real-looking data, not empty states. 3) They get you to invite one teammate within the first session. Happy to share the full spreadsheet if anyone wants it.

Day 847 of building a PM tool:

Customers want simplicity.
Enterprise buyers want complexity.

The tension is the product.

Unpopular opinion: your content calendar doesn't need to be complicated.

3 pillars. 5 posts/week. That's it.

I tried the 'content matrix' approach: 12 content types, 4 audience segments, 3 platforms, weekly themes, monthly campaigns.

Result? Analysis paralysis. I posted twice in January.

Then I simplified:
→ Monday + Wednesday: Educational (how we build)
→ Tuesday + Thursday: Personal (founder lessons)
→ Friday: Hot take or numbers

Output went from 2 posts/month to 20.
Engagement went up 3x.

Complexity is the enemy of consistency.

What does your posting rhythm look like?

#contentmarketing #linkedin #startups

Founders: stop building features. Start building habits.

The tool your team uses every day at 9am wins. Even if it only does one thing.

The real reason your team doesn't use your PM tool 💀

It's not that it's bad.
It's that it asks too much.

Every field. Every dropdown. Every required status update is friction between your team and their actual work.

The fix isn't training. It's fewer fields.

#projectmanagement #startup #productivity #teamwork

The best sales email I ever got was from a founder who said:

'I know you're using [competitor]. Here's the one thing we do that they literally can't.'

One line. Specific. Honest about the competitive landscape.

I replied in 4 minutes.

I almost shut down FlowPM in November.

Cash: 8 weeks of runway.
Churn: 11% monthly.
Morale: I was sleeping on the office couch because going home felt like giving up.

I called my cofounder and said: 'We have two months to fix this or we're done.'

What happened next surprised me.

Instead of panicking, we got brutally focused:
→ Killed every feature below 5% usage
→ Called our 20 happiest customers and asked 'what would make you pay double?'
→ Built exactly what they asked for in 3 weeks

December MRR: $18K → January MRR: $31K
Churn dropped from 11% to 4.8%

The lesson: constraints don't kill startups. Denial does.

When you admit how bad it is, you can finally fix it.

#startups #saas #buildinpublic #founders

Wrote a tweet. Deleted it. Wrote it again. Deleted it again. Wrote 'your Gantt chart is fiction' and got 2K likes.

Content strategy is mostly luck wearing a strategy costume.

How I plan a month of content in 2 hours ⏱️

Step 1: Brain dump every topic I could write about (3 min, no filter)
Step 2: Pick the 12 best
Step 3: Assign to platforms (X gets hot takes, LinkedIn gets stories, IG gets frameworks)
Step 4: Write hooks for each
Step 5: Schedule the first week

That's it. The rest is execution.

📌 Save this for your next planning session

#contentcreator #socialmediatips #startup #marketing #contentplanning

Genuine question for SaaS founders: how do you handle feature requests from your biggest customer when they conflict with your product vision? We have one customer that's 15% of our MRR and they want custom fields, custom workflows, basically a custom product. Every time we say no, our champion there gets a little quieter. Every time we say yes, we add complexity that hurts everyone else. There has to be a middle ground but I haven't found it yet. What's worked for you?

Update: we just passed 500 paying teams.

0-100: took 8 months
100-300: took 4 months
300-500: took 6 weeks

Compounding is real but it takes longer to start than anyone tells you.

7 things I learned from 500 customer calls this year:

1. The 'how are you?' opener kills demos. Jump straight to their problem.
2. Customers who mention competitors are actually warmer — they're actively evaluating.
3. The question 'what would make you switch?' reveals more than any survey.
4. End of quarter buying is real but end of budget-year buying is 10x more powerful.
5. The best customers found us through word of mouth, not ads.
6. Price objections are almost never about price. They're about perceived risk.
7. A 15-minute demo that solves one problem beats a 45-minute demo that shows everything.

Which of these surprises you most?

#sales #saas #startups #b2b

POV: you're a startup founder and your biggest competitor just raised $50M

*opens laptop*
*stares at MRR dashboard*
*closes laptop*
*opens LinkedIn*
*sees their CEO posting about 'the journey'*
*closes everything*

I'm building a PM tool in a market with 10,000 competitors and somehow I've never been more confident.

Because 9,990 of them are trying to be everything for everyone.

I just need to be perfect for 10-person teams.

Your project management tool should feel like a to-do list, not an airplane cockpit ✈️

Swipe to see the difference →

Slide 1: What most PM tools look like (12 tabs, 47 fields, 3 nested menus)
Slide 2: What yours should look like (tasks, people, deadlines — that's it)
Slide 3: The features you think you need vs the features you actually use
Slide 4: Why simpler tools get higher adoption
Slide 5: The 3-field rule for any new tool

#projectmanagement #startup #productivity #design #ux

I got rejected by 23 investors before one said yes.

Not 3. Not 10. Twenty-three.

The first few hurt. By rejection 15, I stopped taking it personally.

By rejection 20, something shifted.

I stopped pitching my product. I started pitching the problem.

Instead of 'FlowPM is a simple project management tool,' I said:

'10-person teams waste 5 hours/week on project management overhead. We make that zero.'

The investor who said yes told me: 'Every founder pitches features. You pitched a pain I've felt myself.'

3 lessons from 23 rejections:
1. Your pitch isn't about your product. It's about their pain.
2. Rejection is data, not a verdict.
3. The 24th door only opens because you knocked on the first 23.

What's the rejection that taught you the most?

#fundraising #startups #founders #venturecapital

1/ Thread: Why I stopped using Jira and built my own PM tool.

This is not a success story. This is a cautionary tale about scratching your own itch so hard you accidentally start a company.

Raised $0 in funding. Built a PM tool. 500+ paying teams. $42K MRR.

Bootstrapping isn't a consolation prize. It's a competitive advantage.

Most founders spend 80% of their time building and 20% distributing.

Flip it.

Your product is only as good as the number of people who know it exists.

I learned this the hard way. For 9 months, I built features. Beautiful features. Features with animations and empty state illustrations.

Know how many people saw them? Our team of 3.

Then I spent one month doing nothing but distribution:
→ 50 cold DMs/day on LinkedIn
→ 3 Reddit posts/week answering PM questions
→ 1 Twitter thread/week showing what we shipped

Month 10 revenue was 4x month 9.

The product didn't change. The distribution did.

Stop building. Start distributing.

What's your distribution channel that actually works?

#startups #distribution #marketing #saas

Every PM tool promises to 'streamline your workflow.'

None of them can explain what that means.

(Including mine, tbh. Working on it.)

Founder confession: I check MRR before I check my messages every morning 📊

Is this healthy? No.
Will I stop? Also no.

Double tap if you're the same 😅

#founderlife #startup #saas #entrepreneur #buildinpublic

We just hit $42K MRR bootstrapped (PM tool for small teams) - AMA. Quick context: started building 18 months ago, launched 14 months ago. Team of 3 (me + 2 engineers). No funding, no marketing budget beyond $200/mo on a few tools. Our entire growth came from: content marketing (LinkedIn + Twitter + Reddit), word of mouth from happy customers, and one viral Product Hunt launch that gave us our first 100 users. Happy to answer anything about bootstrapping, PM tools, or why I still can't afford to pay myself a normal salary despite $42K MRR.
If your standup takes more than 8 minutes, it's not a standup. It's a meeting wearing a standup costume.

I fired our biggest customer last month.

They were 15% of our MRR. They wanted:
→ Custom fields for everything
→ A dedicated Slack channel with our engineers
→ Weekly roadmap reviews where they voted on features

We said no to all three.

They said 'then we're leaving.'

I said 'I understand.'

Here's why:

Every custom feature for one customer is technical debt for 500 others. Every hour our engineers spend on one account is an hour they don't spend making the product better for everyone.

The month after they left:
→ Support tickets dropped 30%
→ Engineering velocity doubled
→ We shipped the feature that 200 other customers had been asking for

Sometimes the best customer decision is losing your biggest customer.

Have you ever fired a customer? How did it go?

#saas #startups #customersuccess #productmanagement

Startup idea validation in 2026:

Step 1: Post about the problem on Twitter
Step 2: If 0 people care, pivot
Step 3: If 10+ people DM you, build it
Step 4: If 100+ people quote tweet you, someone already built it

Things I'd tell myself 18 months ago when I started building FlowPM:

1. Your first 10 customers matter more than your next 10,000
2. Ship weekly or you'll ship never
3. Nobody cares about your feature list. They care about their problem.
4. The best marketing is being useful on the internet for free
5. You'll feel behind every single day. That's not a bug. That's the game.

📌 Save this and come back when you need a reminder

#founderadvice #startup #buildinpublic #saas #entrepreneurship
Every time I add an 'advanced settings' page, an angel loses its wings.

We tracked every support ticket for 6 months. The results surprised me.

📊 Top 5 ticket categories:
1. 'How do I do X?' — 34% (bad UX, not bad users)
2. 'Can you add Y?' — 23% (feature requests disguised as support)
3. 'Something broke' — 18% (real bugs)
4. 'How do I cancel?' — 15% (churn signal, not support)
5. 'Can I talk to someone?' — 10% (lonely founders who want to chat)

The insight: 57% of our support load wasn't about bugs or issues. It was about UX and product gaps.

We didn't hire more support reps. We redesigned the three most confusing screens.

Support volume dropped 40% in one month.

What's your most common support ticket? I bet it's not what you think.

#saas #customersupport #ux #startups

The hardest part of being a solo founder isn't the work.

It's having nobody to high-five when something works.

POV: you finally replaced your 47-tab project management setup with one simple tool 😮‍💨

The before: Notion + Jira + Trello + Google Sheets + Slack reminders + sticky notes + 'I'll remember it'

The after: One tool. Three views. Zero chaos.

Tag someone who needs to see this 👇

#projectmanagement #productivity #startup #saas #organization

I've been building in public for 14 months. Here's the real scorecard:

Followers gained: 4,200
DMs from potential customers: ~60
Actual customers from DMs: 8
Revenue from those 8: $14,400/yr

Not viral. Not impressive. But real.

Building in public is a slow game. Play it anyway.

Stop copying HubSpot's content strategy.

I mean it.

HubSpot has 200+ content writers. A $1B brand. Distribution baked into their domain authority.

You have... a laptop and a dream.

When you copy their playbook — long-form SEO blogs, gated ebooks, nurture sequences — you're playing a game you can't win.

Here's what actually works at our stage:

→ Founder's voice on social (not brand voice)
→ Real numbers (our MRR, our churn, our mistakes)
→ Answering questions on Reddit where our customers already hang out
→ One genuinely useful tool or template (ours: a free PM assessment quiz)

Total content budget: $0
Results: 40% of new signups cite 'saw you on LinkedIn/Twitter'

Your unfair advantage isn't budget. It's that you can be real.

What's your #1 organic growth channel right now?

#contentmarketing #startups #saas #marketing

Tools I actually use to run a SaaS company:

- FlowPM (obviously)
- Linear for bugs
- Cal.com for scheduling
- Stripe for payments
- Plausible for analytics
- A Google Sheet I call 'the war room'

Total cost: $247/mo
Total tools that spark joy: 2

Does anyone else feel like the PM tool market is completely saturated? I'm building in this space and genuinely wondering if there's room for another player. We focus exclusively on teams under 15 people — no Gantt charts, no resource allocation, no enterprise features. Just tasks, deadlines, and who's doing what. We have 500+ paying teams and growing but I still wonder if we're just one pivot away from irrelevance. Would love to hear from other founders building in crowded markets.

Controversial: the best PM tool for a 5-person startup is literally a whiteboard and a daily 'what are you working on?' message.

Tools are for when the whiteboard stops working. Not before.

Month 14 build-in-public update 📊

MRR: $42,000
Customers: 520 teams
Churn: 4.8%
Team: 3 people
Funding: $0

Biggest win this month: shipped async standups. Customers love it.
Biggest miss: tried to add time tracking. Nobody used it. Killed it in 2 weeks.

Onward 🚀

#buildinpublic #saas #startup #mrr #bootstrapped

A framework for deciding what to build next:

1. What do customers ask for most? (demand)
2. What can we build in 2 weeks? (effort)
3. What moves our north star metric? (impact)

If it scores high on all 3, build it. If not, say 'great idea, maybe later' and move on.

The biggest myth in SaaS: 'If you build it, they will come.'

No. They won't.

I built 14 features in our first year. Know which one got us the most customers?

None of them.

What got us customers was me answering PM questions on Reddit every morning for 6 months. No links. No pitches. Just genuine help.

People started DMing me: 'What tool do you use?'

That's when I'd mention FlowPM.

150+ customers came from Reddit. Not from a single ad, blog post, or cold email.

Distribution isn't a channel. It's a habit.

What's your most surprising customer acquisition channel?

new feature shipped: async standups

no more 'can everyone unmute?' at 9am

just type what you're working on when you start your day

that's it

that's the feature

When someone asks 'what makes your PM tool different?' and you have to resist saying 'we have fewer features and that IS the point' 😂

The hardest sell in SaaS: convincing people that less is more.

#projectmanagement #saas #startup #minimalism #product

Sharing our full tech stack for running a $42K MRR SaaS with 3 people — maybe useful for other bootstrapped founders. Backend: Node.js + PostgreSQL on Railway ($45/mo). Frontend: Next.js on Vercel (free tier still). Payments: Stripe. Auth: Clerk. Email: Resend. Analytics: Plausible ($9/mo). Monitoring: BetterStack free tier. PM: our own tool (dogfooding). Support: plain email, no helpdesk software. Total infra cost: ~$120/mo. Total tools: ~$250/mo. We've tried to keep it as lean as possible. Happy to answer questions about any of these choices.
